---
title: "Client Report - Show me More!"
subtitle: "Unit 4 Task 4 (Stretch)"
author: "Vincent Gentry"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

```{python}
# Load the primary ML-ready dataset
url_ml = "https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4dwellings/raw/master/data-raw/dwellings_ml/dwellings_ml.csv"
dwellings = pl.read_csv(url_ml)

# Load the neighborhood dataset
url_nbhd = "https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4dwellings/raw/master/data-raw/dwellings_neighborhoods_ml/dwellings_neighborhoods_ml.csv"
neighborhoods = pl.read_csv(url_nbhd)
```

---

## STRETCH QUESTION 1

**Join the `dwellings_neighborhoods_ml.csv` data to `dwellings_ml.csv` on the `parcel` column and re-run the recommended model (Random Forest). Explain the differences and if this changes the model you recommend to the Client.**

The original dataset only knows stuff about the house itself, like how big it is or what it's made of. The neighborhood dataset adds info about the area around the house. The idea is that some parts of Denver were mostly built up before 1980, so knowing which neighborhood a house is in might help the model make a better guess.

```{python}
# Deduplicate neighborhoods so each parcel appears once, then join
neighborhoods_dedup = neighborhoods.unique(subset=["parcel"], keep="first")
combined = dwellings.join(neighborhoods_dedup, on="parcel", how="left")

# Feature sets (exclude non-feature columns)
exclude = {"parcel", "yrbuilt", "before1980"}

features_base = [c for c in dwellings.columns   if c not in exclude]
features_comb = [c for c in combined.columns    if c not in exclude]

# Cast any remaining string columns to numeric (one-hot already done in source data)
def to_numpy_features(df, feature_list):
    return df.select(feature_list).fill_null(0).to_numpy()

X_base = to_numpy_features(dwellings, features_base)
X_comb = to_numpy_features(combined,  features_comb)
y       = dwellings["before1980"].to_numpy()

# Use the same random split for fair comparison
X_base_train, X_base_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_base, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42
)
X_comb_train, X_comb_test, _, _ = train_test_split(
    X_comb, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42
)

# Train identical Random Forest configs on both datasets
rf_base = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
rf_base.fit(X_base_train, y_train)
y_pred_base = rf_base.predict(X_base_test)
y_prob_base = rf_base.predict_proba(X_base_test)[:, 1]

rf_comb = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
rf_comb.fit(X_comb_train, y_train)
y_pred_comb = rf_comb.predict(X_comb_test)
y_prob_comb = rf_comb.predict_proba(X_comb_test)[:, 1]

# Print side-by-side metrics
for label, y_pred, y_prob in [
    ("Baseline (no neighborhoods)", y_pred_base, y_prob_base),
    ("With neighborhoods joined",   y_pred_comb, y_prob_comb),
]:
    acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    auc = metrics.roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)
    print(f"{label}")
    print(f"  Accuracy : {acc:.4f}")
    print(f"  ROC-AUC  : {auc:.4f}\n")
```

```{python}
# Classification reports for both
for label, clf_model, feat_list, X_test_arr, y_pred, y_prob in [
    ("Baseline",      rf_base, features_base, X_base_test, y_pred_base, y_prob_base),
    ("+ Neighborhoods", rf_comb, features_comb, X_comb_test, y_pred_comb, y_prob_comb),
]:
    print(f"{'='*60}")
    print(f"  Random Forest — {label}")
    print(f"{'='*60}")
    print(metrics.classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["1980+", "Pre-1980"]))
    print(f"ROC-AUC: {metrics.roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob):.4f}\n")

    perm = permutation_importance(clf_model, X_test_arr, y_test, n_repeats=5, random_state=42, n_jobs=1)
    imp_df = (
        pl.DataFrame({"feature": feat_list, "importance": perm.importances_mean.tolist()})
        .sort("importance", descending=True)
        .head(10)
    )
    chart = (
        ggplot(
            {"feature": imp_df["feature"].to_list(), "importance": imp_df["importance"].to_list()},
            aes(x="feature", y="importance")
        )
        + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#2171b5")
        + coord_flip()
        + labs(
            x="Feature",
            y="Mean Decrease in Accuracy",
            title=f"Top 10 Permutation Importances — {label}"
        )
    )
    chart.show()
```

**What I found:** Adding the neighborhood data does seem to help, but only a little. But this makes sense because if a whole neighborhood was built in the 1960s, that's a pretty strong hint that a specific house in it is also from the 1960s. If the accuracy and ROC-AUC both go up noticeably (even just by half a percent or so), I'd tell the client to use the joined version since the extra data is free and easy to include. If the numbers barely move, it's probably not worth the added complexity and I'd just use the original model.

---

## STRETCH QUESTION 2

**Build a regression model that predicts the year a house was built. Report appropriate evaluation metrics.**

Up until now we've only asked the model a yes/no question, such as, was it built before 1980? Now im going to ask it to guess the actual year. This is a regression problem instead of a classification problem, so I switch over to a Random Forest Regressor. Its the same general idea (a bunch of decision trees working together), but instead of outputting "yes" or "no" it spits out a number like 1974.

```{python}
# Use the baseline feature set (no neighborhoods) for simplicity
features_reg = [c for c in dwellings.columns if c not in {"parcel", "yrbuilt", "before1980"}]

X_reg = dwellings.select(features_reg).to_numpy()
y_reg = dwellings["yrbuilt"].to_numpy()

X_reg_train, X_reg_test, y_reg_train, y_reg_test = train_test_split(
    X_reg, y_reg, test_size=0.30, random_state=42
)

rf_reg = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)
rf_reg.fit(X_reg_train, y_reg_train)
y_reg_pred = rf_reg.predict(X_reg_test)
```

```{python}
# Regression evaluation metrics
mae  = metrics.mean_absolute_error(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(metrics.mean_squared_error(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred))
r2   = metrics.r2_score(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred)

print(f"Mean Absolute Error (MAE) : {mae:.2f} years")
print(f"Root Mean Squared Error   : {rmse:.2f} years")
print(f"R²                        : {r2:.4f}")
```

```{python}
# Actual vs Predicted scatter plot
sample_idx = np.random.default_rng(42).choice(len(y_reg_test), size=500, replace=False)
actual_s    = y_reg_test[sample_idx].tolist()
predicted_s = y_reg_pred[sample_idx].tolist()

chart_scatter = (
    ggplot({"actual": actual_s, "predicted": predicted_s}, aes(x="actual", y="predicted"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.4, color="#2171b5", size=2)
    + geom_abline(slope=1, intercept=0, color="red", linetype="dashed")
    + labs(
        x="Actual Year Built",
        y="Predicted Year Built",
        title="Regression: Actual vs. Predicted Year Built (500-row sample)"
    )
)
chart_scatter.show()
```

```{python}
# Residual distribution
residuals = (y_reg_test - y_reg_pred).tolist()

chart_resid = (
    ggplot({"residual": residuals}, aes(x="residual"))
    + geom_histogram(bins=50, fill="#2171b5", color="white")
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="red", linetype="dashed")
    + labs(
        x="Residual (Actual − Predicted)",
        y="Count",
        title="Residual Distribution — Year Built Regression"
    )
)
chart_resid.show()
```

```{python}
# Top feature importances for the regression model
perm_reg = permutation_importance(rf_reg, X_reg_test, y_reg_test, n_repeats=5, random_state=42, n_jobs=1)
imp_reg_df = (
    pl.DataFrame({"feature": features_reg, "importance": perm_reg.importances_mean.tolist()})
    .sort("importance", descending=True)
    .head(10)
)
chart_imp = (
    ggplot(
        {"feature": imp_reg_df["feature"].to_list(), "importance": imp_reg_df["importance"].to_list()},
        aes(x="feature", y="importance")
    )
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#238b45")
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        x="Feature",
        y="Mean Increase in MSE",
        title="Top 10 Permutation Importances — Year Built Regressor"
    )
)
chart_imp.show()
```

**What the numbers mean:**

- **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)** is probably the easiest one to understand since it's just the average number of years the model was off. So if MAE is 8, the model's guesses were off by about 8 years on average. That's not bad when you think about how hard it is to guess a year just from square footage and style.
- **RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)** is similar to MAE but it punishes big mistakes extra. If RMSE is way higher than MAE, it means a handful of predictions were really far off like guessing 2000 for a house actually built in 1955. That can happen when an old house got heavily remodeled.
- **R²** is basically "how much of the variation in year built does the model actually explain?" An R² of 0.80 means the model accounts for 80% of why years differ between houses. Higher is better, if it's below 0.5 the model is just guessing.
- The **scatter plot** shows actual year on the x-axis and what the model predicted on the y-axis. If it were a perfect model, every dot would be right on the red dashed line. The closer the dots are to that line, the better.
- The **residual histogram** shows how wrong the model was for each house. If it's roughly centered on zero, that means the model isn't consistently guessing too high or too low and it's just normally wrong in both directions, which is what you want.

Honestly, for what the client actually needs (figuring out which houses might have asbestos), the yes/no classifier from the last task is more useful. Knowing a house was "probably built around 1972" is cool, but knowing "it was almost definitely built before 1980" is more actionable and useful in my opinion. The regression model is a nice bonus but I wouldn't swap out the classifier for it.
